Persisted Pin/layout state must stay serializable and portable rather than tied to one browser storage implementation.

decision_log.decision: Use an L-level complete phased plan
decision_log.reason: The user explicitly rejected MVP thinking; the work spans measurement, graph-engine semantics, workbench drawer behavior, browser regressions, degradation budgets, and large-renderer trial boundaries.

decision_log.decision: Move semantic/drawer contracts before click behavior changes
decision_log.reason: Node/community click semantics cannot be cleanly verified until lightweight drawer states and commands exist.

decision_log.decision: Phase 1 must measure 5000 and 10000 graph shapes
decision_log.reason: 1000 nodes are a baseline, not the large-graph target; failure at 5000/10000 is evidence and must be captured early.

decision_log.decision: Run strong-prior three-route global renderer trial
decision_log.reason: Sigma/Graphology is the first WebGL candidate, vis-network validates the Canvas route, and no-new-dependency aggregation is the fallback; only one production global route may survive.

decision_log.decision: Keep desktop-app compatibility as an architecture guardrail
decision_log.reason: Future desktop form should reuse graph semantics instead of creating a separate product logic layer.

decision_log.decision: Force ledger inclusion before implementation proceeds
decision_log.reason: The new plan and progress files live under ignored docs/plans, so Phase 0 must prove they are intentionally tracked.

decision_log.decision: Performance phases cannot pass without measurement artifacts
decision_log.reason: Large-graph smoothness cannot be proven by prose; browser fallback can support functional checks, not replace performance data.

decision_log.decision: Use fixed performance records in the progress ledger
decision_log.reason: Later renderer and budget decisions need comparable numbers, not only evidence links.

decision_log.decision: Add a disposable validation gate and install necessary trial dependencies when needed
decision_log.reason: Risky unknowns should be proven in isolated throwaway harnesses before product-code implementation; the user approved installing packages needed to make the plan proceed.

decision_log.decision: Allow aggregation-first only as a staged selected route
decision_log.reason: Short-term aggregation and long-term renderer integration may be a sequence, but the product must not keep two parallel global graph experiences.

decision_log.decision: Keep DOM/SVG for community reading but stop treating it as the final 10000+ global renderer
decision_log.reason: The current implementation uses button nodes and SVG path edges, which is still appropriate for reading and scoped community views but not proven for 5000+/10000+ global browsing.

decision_log.decision: First-stage fallback organization is core structure / connectivity only
decision_log.reason: Type/source/time organization can become a second graph system and depends on metadata quality.

decision_log.decision: Edge click relationship inspection remains out of scope
decision_log.reason: The first stage keeps edges passive while retaining strong relationship summaries in node/community drawers.

decision_log.decision: Compare Sigma/Graphology WebGL, vis-network Canvas, and no-new-dependency aggregation fallback with one production global route only
decision_log.reason: The plan must test the main rendering candidates without splitting the product into multiple permanent global experiences.

decision_log.decision: Allow aggregation-first only as a staged route if it wins, not as a parallel permanent product
decision_log.reason: Aggregation may be the chosen global route, but it must not sit beside a second global renderer in production.

decision_log.decision: Record explicit dependency approval before trial packages are installed
decision_log.reason: Candidate renderer packages need a written approval boundary before package changes land.

decision_log.decision: Use Phase 4.1 shared render budgets for DOM/SVG output control
decision_log.reason: Phase 1.3 measured DOM/SVG update volume as the large-graph bottleneck: 10000-node global view created about 52649 DOM nodes and timed out during pan/node click. The first budget layer must stop full-card global rendering, cap edges and labels, and expose overflow to drawer/list state without pretending DOM/SVG is the final 10000+ route.

decision_log.decision: Separate stable graph importance from temporary interaction boosts
decision_log.reason: Search, selection, and Pin should make objects easier to find without changing the durable core anchors and skeleton edges that make the map feel stable. Stable structure is now based on graph structure and community representatives; temporary boosts are reported separately and only affect budget competition.

decision_log.decision: Use measured community focus size bands before renderer-route selection
decision_log.reason: Phase 1.3 showed oversized community focus can create large DOM output and slow drawer/community transitions. Community focus now has explicit small/medium/large/oversized behavior so scoped reading stays rich only when the community is actually small enough.
decision_log.source: task 4.3 implementation and Phase 1.3 DOM/SVG baseline

decision_log.decision: Interaction-time degradation is a shared budget rule plus DOM renderer state, not a separate product mode
decision_log.reason: Pan, zoom, drag, and search need to reduce ordinary labels, weak edges, and heavy effects while preserving selected, searched, pinned, and stable core anchors; the same semantics must remain portable to later Canvas/WebGL or desktop shells.

decision_log.entry: Task 5.1 preserved graph layout for search, type filters, and drawer/return-global flows by updating lightweight visual state instead of rebuilding layout. The ledger advanced to Phase 5 task 5.2.

decision_log.decision: Task 6.1 dependency approval: install sigma@3.0.3 and graphology@0.26.0 only for isolated Sigma/Graphology WebGL renderer trial harness; no production adoption until Phase 6.4 route decision.
decision_log.reason: Phase 6.1 requires the approved first-candidate WebGL trial and the user already approved installing necessary trial dependencies.

decision_log.decision: Retain Sigma/Graphology as a measured Phase 6 candidate, not a production route yet
decision_log.reason: The isolated trial passed all required 1000/5000/10000/oversized shapes with constant DOM output and preserved shared graph semantics, but Phase 6.2, 6.3, and 6.4 still must compare alternatives before one production global route is selected.

decision_log.decision: Task 6.2 dependency approval: install vis-network only for isolated Canvas renderer comparison trial; no production adoption until Phase 6.4 route decision.
decision_log.reason: Phase 6.2 requires the approved Canvas comparison route and the user already approved installing necessary trial dependencies.

decision_log.decision: Retain vis-network as a measured Canvas candidate with semantic ownership risks
decision_log.reason: The isolated Canvas trial passed all required shapes and stayed smooth on wheel zoom, but search, point select, and container/community updates were slower than Sigma/Graphology and production integration must prevent vis-network callbacks/layout state from owning llm-wiki semantics.

decision_log.decision: Retain aggregation fallback as a fast structural fallback, not proof of full rich 10000+ exploration
decision_log.reason: The no-new-dependency trial passed required shapes with fast initial render and 60 FPS zoom, but it caps labels/cards/edges and hides thousands of interaction-time objects on 5000/10000 shapes, so it is best treated as a fallback or staged structural overview unless Phase 6.4 chooses it explicitly.

decision_log.decision: Select Sigma/Graphology as the single future global large-graph renderer route
decision_log.reason: Sigma/Graphology passed all required shapes, kept wheel zoom near 60 FPS through the 10000-node shape, had faster semantic search/selection/container updates than vis-network, and supports richer direct global exploration than aggregation-only while preserving shared graph semantics through the adapter contract.

rejected_alternatives.vis-network: Rejected as primary route because semantic update actions were slower and integration has higher risk of renderer-owned selection/layout behavior conflicting with graph-engine semantics.
rejected_alternatives.aggregation-fallback: Rejected as standalone route because it intentionally caps labels, cards, and edges and hides thousands of interaction-time objects at large sizes; retained as degradation strategy.
rejected_alternatives.current-dom-svg: Rejected as final global renderer because Phase 1 recorded 10000-node and oversized-community performance failures; retained for small graph and community reading.

Task 3.3 completed blank click/double-click/fixed-position semantics. Browser regression now proves true blank double-click returns global while node, community wash, edge, toolbar, legend, search, and drawer double-clicks do not; explicit fixed/unfixed drawer actions remain available.

Task 3.4 completed return-global state preservation. Return global now fits the global graph while preserving search, filters, selection, Pin/fixed positions, and lightweight node/community summary state; reset layout remains separate and only clears fixed positions.

Task 4.1 added shared global/community render budgets. Global view now renders zero full node cards, labels/edges/cards/interaction updates are capped, selected/search/Pin signals only promote priority within caps, and overflow is reported for drawer/list use.

Task 4.2 split stable importance from temporary boosts. Stable core anchors and skeleton edges remain unchanged across search/selection changes, while search hits, selected objects, and Pin nodes can still win limited visible labels within budget.

Task 4.3 added community focus size bands. Small, medium, large, and oversized communities now have distinct budgeted outputs; oversized communities expose an internal-map-entry representation instead of rendering every member as a card.

Task 4.4 added interaction-time degradation. Pan, zoom, drag, and search now enter a lightweight active mode that fades weak edges, hides ordinary labels, reduces effects, preserves selected/search/pinned/core anchors, and restores idle detail after interaction settles. Phase 4 is complete and the ledger advanced to Phase 5 task 5.1.
